critic_raw:
ASSESSOR 1:
Verdict: critique_wins

Time horizon mismatch is the primary issue: best-of-window for ML vs. single-point for qSOFA. Feature availability is secondary: late-arriving labs in ML predictions vs. triage-time-only for qSOFA. Purpose mismatch is tertiary: surveillance deployment claim vs. triage baseline comparison. Together these issues substantially inflate the 0.15 AUROC gap.
defender_raw:
ASSESSOR 2:
Verdict: critique_wins

The study is internally inconsistent: it proposes continuous surveillance deployment but evaluates against a triage-point baseline. This is the clearest problem. Additionally, the evaluation design gives the ML model a window-selection advantage. These are design flaws, not statistical uncertainties.

ASSESSOR 3:
Verdict: critique_wins

Three independent methodological advantages for the ML model compound to produce the 0.15 AUROC gap: time window selection, information availability post-triage, and comparing a surveillance tool against a triage tool. Each individually would inflate the reported advantage; together they make the 0.15 gap unreliable as a measure of model quality.
adjudication_raw: Unanimous critique_wins. All three assessors identified the same structural issues. The verdict is clear and well-supported.
